sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
All right.
So I-- so the first transport went-- went out on-- on-- on May-- let's say May 19, 1944.
And it was a sight.
When I saw the people in the first transport, people-- and men, women, and children everything.
And I come from a very religious home and I was only a young boy.
And I said, there is no God.
To see a sight like this-- you can read as many books as you want.
This is unbelievable, to see a site like this.
Little was I to realize that I'm going to be in the same-- same group, only a week later.
But it's different when you're in it, or when you see it.
Somehow when you're in it, you don't-- it doesn't have that-- that impact like seeing it the first time.
Of course, we didn't know where they were going.
The were taken-- they were taken away.
As soon as they were taken out, a third of the ghetto was empty.
I went into that third and I found some very nice suits with shirts and everything because people left it behind.
And I dressed up like a millionaire.
I looked like a rich man.
[LAUGHS] For the first time I felt like I am rich because they had all kinds of food.
They left a lot of stuff behind in the-- in the third section.
A week later, on a Friday, it was, I think, May 26, it was-- exactly end of-- no, it was shul was Sunday and Monday, and this was just the Friday before shul.
Our turn came.
Again, I was part of the same mass of people, the second section.
And then I know what happened.
They took us to the synagogue.
And they took away, again, if they found anything left on us, they took it away.
And there already they were looking brutal, because there was one incident [SIGHING] where they kept-- there were Germans there already.
They said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You know, go fast.
And for some reason I, like, I got a little bit lost.
And this Nazi he hit me with, like with a baseball bat on my-- on my back.
And he sort of knocked the wind out of me.
I couldn't breathe.
And my mother was behind me and she gave me a push.
And I looked back and I saw my mother.
And I-- I saw-- I saw-- I know I saw her face.
She was helpless.
And it's another sight which is very hard for me to--to forget.
And so we were pushed along and we were taken out to the railroad station, in [NON-ENGLISH].
And over there there were wagons, cattle cars, waiting for us.
And they started to load them up.
As people came, they started to load up car number one, and so on.
And in every car where I went in there was like a couple of loaves of bread.
And there was a pail of water-- fresh, I mean water.
And one pail was like for sanitary purposes.
And we were jammed into that-- you-- you-- you people couldn't lay down.
I want you to know that my great grandmother, my great grandmother, that I told you before, she was in the same transport.
When she came to the ghetto the only thing she took along was her [NON-ENGLISH], because she made her own [NON-ENGLISH]-- her own [NON-ENGLISH].
And this is the only thing she held on to.
Even when they took us out to the train, she held onto this bundle.
In case she died, she wanted to be buried in the [NON-ENGLISH], in the [NON-ENGLISH].
And they-- people were dying in the train, too, because there was no room.
It was-- everybody was jammed in.
And there were fights breaking out-- people didn't get along.
Because, you know, to stay crammed up like this.
Every-- [SIGHS] And then they closed the doors.
They closed the doors.
There were two sliding doors.
Or one sliding door.
They closed it.
And in the top there was a little window.
The window must have been maybe about-- about maybe 12, 14 inches.
Maybe about 20 inches or 2 feet long by 12, 14 inches high.
And under the window there was barbed wire, you know.
And I was near the window and I would sometimes pull myself up and look out.
You know, I could look out.
And we left [NON-ENGLISH], that-- I would say, around 1 o'clock.
And we arrived midnight to a place called Kosice.
Kosice, this is what it's called in Slovakia.
Today it's partly-- today it's the second largest city in Slovakia.
But in Hungarian it was called Kassa.
All the trains from Hungary going to Auschwitz went through Kassa.
And again, that-- the middle of the night, we heard in Hungarian, first, anybody has jewelry, money, silverware has to get rid of what.
If anybody is caught with it is going to be shot forthwith.
And about 15 minutes later we heard the same story-- anybody with jewelry.
But this time, it wasn't spoken in Hungarian.
This time it was spoken in German.
And the Germans took over in Kosice.
They took over the train.

I don't know every train or every second-- I mean every box or every second car, there was like a little [NON-ENGLISH], like a little [NON-ENGLISH] where the soldier was standing up, you know, in the train.
He was standing up where he could have a view.
Every few cars had like a soldier was there, you know.
And that's all you heard in the train, going, go, go, go, go.
Even today, whenever I travel a train, you know-- pop, pop, pop-- that's the first thing that comes back to my mind is-- is-- is-- is the-- is the railroad.
It was going until the next day around the afternoon we reached the place that must have been Slovakia, Slovakia, because I pulled myself up and I saw people which were not Hungarian.
And the fields were beautiful.
This was in May, late May.
Countryside was gorgeous.
And the elderly people in the car would say, which way are we going?
They didn't know where we were going.
But somebody said, we are heading north.
Then someone says, if we are heading north, we're in big trouble.
Because we are heading towards Poland.
We didn't know exactly.
I want you to know that we were in Hungary until 1944.
We still didn't know what the Holocaust-- the Holocaust was going full blast in Poland.
Millions of Jews were killed in Russia, the Ukraine, France, all the occupied countries.
Hungary was the last country.
They came in 1944.
My village, the Russians were in my village that October.
Only six months prior to that, the Russians already were there.
It was the last action of the Germans during World War II.
They lost the war already.
But the biggest atrocities took place when they killed the Hungarian Jews in a matter of-- in a matter of six, seven weeks-- they killed close to half a million Jews in Hungary.
But anyway, in Kosice-- in Slovakia, they opened the door and we had a chance to empty out the buckets, and to fill in some new water.
And [SIGHS] they locked the doors again, and we started to go.
And again, all day, all night was going-- going until a day later.
That was the first day [NON-ENGLISH]-- 1944.
The first day [NON-ENGLISH].
It was like 2 o'clock in the afternoon, and the train pulled in.
And I seen people when I looked through the little bit window I seen people dressed in stripe-- in stripe uniforms.
And they opened up the doors and everybody said, [GERMAN], everybody get out.
And they told us to leave all the bundles on the-- on the-- on the-- what do you call it by the train there?
Platform.
On the platform.
And we said, what?
They said, don't worry.
You're going to get the bundles.
Now everybody got busy starting to write their names and addresses on the bundles, because if we are going to come back to pick up the bundles, we wanted to know which is our bundle.
So everybody got busy with pens, with crayons, they started to write, to write, you know, the name, the address.
Everybody was busy writing.
In the meantime, you know, there were soldiers with dogs and they said, out, everybody, you know.
And then they announced that all men, like from age 15 to 40, 45, I don't remember exactly, to stand on one side.
And then all women a certain age, and all children and mothers with children should keep on going.
And I stood with my brother, because my brother, Mordcha, he was with me.
And as-- as-- as my column was moving along, I saw my mother with my other sister and brother-- and brothers.
And she was like a little teary eyed.
She was like crying.
I don't know if she sensed something-- but really, I don't think anybody knew what's going to happen.
And my brother motioned to me that I should send Mordcha over to her, my little brother.
My brother maybe was a year, two years younger than me-- Mordcha.
And she motioned to me that I should send over Mordcha to her.
So I told Mordcha to go to be with my mother.
And so they all-- we all went one side.
And we-- the men who stayed in a line, we went-- there were a group of officers, German officers.
And there were like three or four officers.
See, one guy had a baton in his hand, and he just motioned.
I'm talking about the men who were between 16 and 45, you know-- able-bodied men.
But even among those men, some people weren't able to work, because first of all the ghetto took a toll.
And the transport-- we got there was-- we were pretty beaten up already, you know, tired.
So I remember my grandfather was with me.
And my grandfather went before the officer stood up erect.
And the officer showed with the baton, this way.
And then I went up, and they showed me this way.
So I went to the right.
The rest of them they went to the left.
I still didn't know what happened.
You know, nobody.
But as soon as they went to the right, they started to-- to-- to chase us to a barrack.
We got-- we took off all our clothes, and we were shaven clean all over.
And they give us the clothes, you know, the striped-- with the striped-- the striped clothes.
And so I had now uniform.
I had a striped uniform with a, you know, like a-- like a chapeau, made from the same material.
Except the only thing we had was our shoes-- the original shoes we still had.
And from there we went into to-- to the gypsy camp.
It was the C lager, [GERMAN] lager.
And the gypsy was sitting there and they were-- they were the gypsies there.
And they had family there, too.
They have children.
And they will take like-- they had a big barrel.
I don't know what they had-- like a [NON-ENGLISH].
And the gypsy would take like a-- with his finger, [NON-ENGLISH], put it in your hand, this is what you ate.
And we all stayed in line.
And they put us into [NON-ENGLISH]-- into a-- into a barrack.
That night they opened up the barracks and they came in.
They were beating, they were shooting, they were killing.
They weren't soldiers-- they must have been some criminals which the Germans apparently put up.
And, of course, as they were hitting the people in the front, people started to push back.
And then when they pushed back, now that we we were set into this barrack, we were a lot of people there, but then they pushed them back.
So there was more room.
So they brought in more people, and they put them in.
And then after a while, you know, you couldn't breathe because the air was-- you know, you couldn't breathe.
And it was very hard to breathe.
It's-- you know-- it's you get terrorized, like, you know, you can't breathe.
A lot of people died that night.
I think that was basic training more or less.
The very able bodied to survive something like this was-- you know.
The following day we were sitting out-- I'm talking about Birkenau.
I was in Birkenau.
The following day we were sitting out on the-- on the-- on the sun.
You know, we were sitting on the floor.
The sun beating up-- beating on us.
No food, and no water.
Water was-- we needed water more than food.
And a day later, and then I remember one, in the middle of the night-- not the middle, they started that night, where they called everybody who-- was named started with an A to come forward.
And when they went forward, they went ahead and they tattooed a number.
Now my name is Rosenthal, so it's an R.
So I came out there like 1 o'clock in the morning.
And you know-- so one guy just went ahead and he held my hand.
And he took a-- a-- a-- like a stick, and he started to-- to punch-- to punch in the number.
So my number was A 11832.
Forget about it-- I didn't have no more name.
This was my number.
You know what is interesting is that most of the people there that night, they were Hungarians.
And those-- and sometimes people, they always think for the better.
They said the reason for the A is because we are Hungarian Jews.
And as Hungarian Jews, we're entitled to better treatment.
We are Hungarian Jews-- like the Hungarian Jews, they were very proud Jews.
They were proud citizens of Hungary, like the German Jews.
We are Hungarian Jews.
But A stands for 200,000.
So I was the prisoner-- by the way, only those prisoners that remain in Auschwitz were numbered.
So I was prisoner 211,832.
That was my number.
And from there I was marched to Auschwitz-- from Birkenau.
And they lined us up on a-- on a-- you know.
And they were driving us, they were driving us to Auschwitz which is the camp in Auschwitz.
And when I saw the sign there [SPEAKING GERMAN], I'll never forget, never forget.
We came into that gate, and then we made a right turn.
And I see the sign, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
I speak Yiddish, I said, oh, if you work, you're going to-- you know, whatever it means.
And I felt a little better.
And we were in Auschwitz.
They lined us up one day.
[LAUGHS] And everybody-- we all have our shoes.
And they took off our shoes, and they pulled off the heels from the shoes.
They pulled off the heels.
Because some people put in money into the heels.
So they pulled off the heels from the shoes, and then they gave me other shoes.
They gave me shoes.
The shoes had wooden-- wooden soles with material on it, you know so.
And from there I was marched to a camp which is called Buna, Buna Monowitz.
Today, Auschwitz is called Auschwitz-I.
Birkenau is called Auschwitz-II, and Buna is called Auschwitz-III.
Buna Monowitz one of it was a big factory which was an IG farm, IG farm from IG farm.
And they employed us as slave labor.
And, you know, IG farm went to the SS, and they-- and they hired us as labor.
And apparently the SS got paid for us, to feed us.
Now, we were staying in Buna.
In the morning, in Buna, you got up and first of all, we had barracks, you know And the barracks were bunks.
They were three in a row, you know.
And they had-- they had like barrels in the middle.
First of all, when I was in Birkenau, they used to have like-- in the barrack, there was long stoves-- long stoves.
About maybe 60, 70 feet long-- that was for heating purposes.
Because at one time, that used to be for horses.
On either side of the stove was a chimney.
When you go back to Auschwitz, you don't see the chimney and those long stoves.
But they used to use those stoves, what they used to do is they used to take somebody and put them in there, and then beat them.
So the stove, I mean this long thing, would muffle the sound out.
But in Buna, we had barracks, regular barracks, because this was, you know-- IG farm had a factory, the Buna factory, where they made synthetic rubber.
And in case you had to go at night to the bathroom, so you went to the barrel-- there were barrels lined up.
And when the barrel was full, [LAUGHS] whoever was lucky enough, you know, then you had to take the barrel out, someplace out-- carry it.
It was heavy.
So some people did not go to the barrel, so-- [LAUGHS] so they peed them on top of the other.
So the guy who was in the bottom, he got the worse part of it.
So everybody want to stay on top, you know.
But this was-- but in Buna already it was different.
In Buna, there was already bathrooms, more or less.
And in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in there was one place where they had showers, you know.
Once a week we go for showers.
You could go for delousing.
Delousing is you took off your clothes and you throw them in there, and-- and they boiled-- they boiled-- you know, they-- they boiled the-- the clothes.
And they gave them back to you.
I'll tell you one thing-- in Auschwitz, in Buna, there were no lice.
You know, there were no-- you know, there was no-- they-- they kept pretty clean there.
The only problem is, you know, in Buna, whenever after a couple of weeks, they would come there for a selection.
In other words, there was come a doctor-- this doctor's name was, in Buna, his name was-- his name was Fisher.
And they would take the whole block and line 'em up.
And whoever was too skinny, you know, they would take them out in the night.
And a couple of days later, they were sent to the gas chamber.
My uncle was sent there, and other-- you know.
And even there, I remember even there, when they-- when they-- when they had a selection to take them away to Auschwitz, and there was a rumor they're going to be gassed.
And there was one boy I knew, he says to me, they're going to go to a sanatorium in order to build us up a little bit.
Even then they wouldn't believe it.
All right, let me tell you--in Buna, no, I did not know there were gas chambers.
No.
No, I didn't know.
The only thing, I'll tell you, and I forgot to tell you.
It's very important.
When I came to Birkenau, and I was in Birkenau, the stench of burning flesh-- you could smell it.
It was like, I don't know, like sometimes they fry chicken.
So you're going to where there is a certain smell to it.
The air was permeated with that stench.
And I remember my mother's brother once picked me up because the barrack had like a little window in the top, and he told me to look out.
And I saw huge flames.
And he says to me, what do you see?
And I said the sky's flame-- the sky's in flames.
Because the flames were tremendous, leaping into the sky.
And he said, our transport is being burned there.
It was unbelievable.
It's unbelievable.
It so happened my mother's brother, my uncle, he went crazy.
He went off his mind.
And I'm telling you, today it's very easy to talk-- you know, sometimes people say this is what you know, you didn't know.
Everybody became a philosopher.
But I'm telling you because I have a very good memory.
People didn't believe that that's going to happen.
Most of the people didn't know what's happening.
Even the ones, who-- they wouldn't believe it.
Because it's something which never happened.
It just-- it just didn't enter somebody's mind.
It just didn't happen.
So I was in-- in-- in Buna and I worked as a-- well, I worked there carrying bricks and mortar and-- and-- and cement.
And sometimes it was very cold, so I would tear open a bag of cement and I take the paper and I put it on behind my jacket, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
And-- this is what it was.
Every morning, I'll tell you-- just simply what it was like this.
In the morning, you get up.
You make your bunk.
Then you go out to appel-- you went to the appelplatz.
You lined up on the appelplatz.
Then, you march out to work.
When you march out to work, there was a band.
The band was playing.
Every morning, there was no music, there was no work.
And the band, they were dressed like Napoleons.
There were about 60, 70 people in the band.
They would march.
And we would line up.
And everybody had like a-- like a-- metal-- a metal [NON-ENGLISH], where you're hanging from the shoulder.
Because this is what was for your soup.
So we were put in-- we had blocks.
And each day we were in blocks.
For example I was in block 30.
I remember, there was other blocks.
That way they went out to work.
It was called kommando-- I was in kommando 90, which was the [NON-ENGLISH] kommando.
This was one of the largest kommando in Buna.
There was one larger one, which is called the [NON-ENGLISH] kommando.
[NON-ENGLISH] kommando was very bad, because they had to put cable into the trenches.
And a lot of them died in the trenches.
It was very rough.
I was in the [NON-ENGLISH] kommando.
I was kommando 90-- [NON-ENGLISH] kommando.
Our kapo was Kapo Carl.
We have four kapos in our kommando.
And then after the kapos, you had four [NON-ENGLISH]-- little, you know, little details.
Everybody had a [NON-ENGLISH].
But the band was playing, you went up to the factories.
You worked in the factory.
Came 12 o'clock, you got soup-- it's called Buna soup.
The first day, it was like soup made out of straw.
The first day.
I look at it, and there were some Greek Jews over there.
And they looked at me and I didn't eat it.
So I gave it to them, and they were so happy, and they drank it.
And then 3 o'clock, I was hungry, very, very hungry.
So I went over to the kapo.
The kapo was a Jewish guy from Poland, a Jewish boy.
And I said to him, I would like to have some soup.
[LAUGHS] And he beat the hell out of-- he beat me up so badly that I think I was psychologically effected to this day.
And I couldn't-- he says to me, what is this?
He says, you're-- you're asking your mother to give you soup?
I was hungry.
And then once a week or so, there used to be a hanging.
The hanging used to be public executions.
I remember there were three boys hung once-- they tried to escape.
And-- so what happened there, when we came back at night, we went to our appel, we stayed in our appel.
They had the gallows out-- could be anywhere from one to three gallows, you know.
And-- and-- you know, the officer from the camp, his name was Schotl-- he came up and he read-- he read the verdict.
And approved by Heinrich Himmler.
So they put him-- you know, they put a rope around them, and they kicked the scaffold from underneath.
And they fell in.
And they were dancing there for about seven, 10 minutes, depending on how-- and then they would die.
You know, and there were-- there were public executions like this.
But all in all, Buna and Auschwitz, when I think back, was like the French Riviera compared to Buchenwald, because hell, that was bad-- Buchenwald.
So I was-- I was in Buna till January 18, 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And see what happened already, the Russian armies they started to approach Auschwitz.
And they had an order to evacuate us.
And that morning, we didn't go out to work.
At that point?
No.
I thought that they are someplace alive.
As a matter of fact, I'll tell you a story.
I was always thinking to myself when I was there, you know, and seen what I'd seen, and things like this, and I was a young boy-- I was 16 years old.
And I always said to myself, this is going to be over.
I'm going to see my mother, and I'm going to see my everybody from my old village.
And I'm going to tell them stories about what happened to me.
They wouldn't believe it.
I always thought to myself that I'm going to meet them and I'm going to tell them a story of what happened to me.
You know it's an unbelievable story.
But I, as far as I'm concerned, and I could tell you that I remember.
I have a good memory.

I don't believe that people knew, even when you were in Auschwitz-- except those that worked in-- there was a kommando, which sonderkommando, Kanada kommando, and they were in charge with taking of the people off the train.
We asked them, and stuff-- they knew.
But even if they would tell it to somebody-- first of all nobody, you know, they wouldn't believe it.
I am telling you, I always hoped-- and I could show you downstairs, I have-- where I searched for my family after the war.
I still thought they are alive.
And I always [LAUGHS] thought to myself, I'm going to meet them, I'm going to tell them a story, you know, what happened.
So January 18th, it was a very cold day.
We didn't go out to work.
And again, I could tell you stories about Buna and so on, but, you know, time is running out.
And they ordered everybody out into the main street of the camp.
And there was a place in Buna which is called a [NON-ENGLISH], where people were in the [NON-ENGLISH].
And some people-- when ahead-- from the-- they were sick.
And they tried to, because they thought they were going to remain behind, they're going to shoot them.
It just so happened by coincidence that whoever remained in the [NON-ENGLISH], the Russians came in and they found them there.
The Russians came in there in January, 1927.
They marched us up.
And by the way in-- January 1927?
January 27, 1945.
And we were taken out from Buna in January 18, 1945, that night.
Now, in Buna, there was one building which was called the whore house.
You know the SS used to have whores.
They were not Jewish girls.
And I remember that night, you know, they had-- you know, they had wagons and the took all the whores [LAUGHS] and they put them in the wagon.
And the officers were with the whores, and we pulled the wagon, you know.
Not me, but I saw, you know, the halfling-- halfling-- when all the prisoners.
They pulled the wagon.
[SIGHS] And they start to march us.
And we went out that night.
And we went to the town of Auschwitz.
I'm talking the town of Auschwitz, not the camp.
Because I remember a guy next to me, he says to me, you see, I used to live there.
He passed through his house.
This guy passed through the house where he used to live.
And he must have been in the camps for years because the Polish Jews were taken there years before we were taken in.
And it's the first time I saw it.
And we passed through Auschwitz.
And they kept on driving us.
And it was snowing.
And I had wooden shoes.
You know, when you march in snow, the snow piles up on the sole.
If it's leather, the snow--
